stories.title: Boston, Army Corps Issue Draft Study of $10B Coastal Resilience Program
stories.excerpt: The City of Boston on Tuesday announced the release of the US Army Corps of Engineers’ (USACE) draft Boston Coastal Storm Risk Management Feasibility Study. Projected for completion in 2028, the study will allow the city to access up to 65% federal cost-sharing in a $10-billion program to construct 20-plus coastal resilience projects and prevent an estimated $41 billion in damages from coastal flooding. “The future of our city depends on the steps we take now to build resilient communities,” said Mayor Michelle Wu. “With more frequent flooding in vulnerable areas during extreme weather events, the Coastal Storm Risk Management Feasibility Study is critical to understanding how to protect inland neighborhoods most at risk and unlocking resources to take action.” Roughly one sixth of Boston is built on filled tidelands and is in danger of flooding due to sea-level rise and extreme weather, according to Wu’s office. Since 1991, Boston has experienced 24 severe storm events that triggered federal or state disaster declarations,14 of which caused major flooding. The draft report is open to public comment from July 24 through Sept. 18. Residents and stakeholders can submit their feedback and learn about upcoming community meetings and webinars here. The post Boston, Army Corps Issue Draft Study of $10B Coastal Resilience Program appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Boston Army Corps draft $10B coastal resilience study; 65% federal cost-share available
One-sixth of Boston built on filled tidelands; 24 severe storms since 1991 triggered disaster declarations
Study completion targeted 2028; opens multiyear window for infrastructure-backed redevelopment projects
stories.summary: The Army Corps of Engineers released a draft Feasibility Study for a $10 billion Boston Coastal Storm Risk Management program, offering up to 65% federal cost-sharing for 20+ resilience projects. Roughly one-sixth of Boston sits on filled tidelands and faces flooding risk; the city has endured 24 severe storms since 1991, 14 causing major flooding. The study will be completed in 2028, unlocking federal funding for coastal protection infrastructure.
stories.impact: This creates a multi-year planning and funding window for mixed-use and residential development in Boston's vulnerable neighborhoods. Developers should monitor federal appropriations and the final study (due 2028) to identify areas receiving resilience investments—these zones will see improved infrastructure, reduced flood risk, and potential tax incentives, making them more financeable for new construction and value-add projects. Inland neighborhoods most at risk may become attractive infill targets once infrastructure commitments are clear.

stories.title: AI Company Mercor Takes 26K SF at Durst’s One World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: One World Trade Center is almost 100 percent leased thanks to the latest deal with an artificial intelligence company, Commercial Observer has learned. Mercor, which employs human experts to train AI algorithms, has inked a five-year, 25,550-square-foot deal on the 77th floor of the Lower Manhattan office tower, the Durst Organization announced Tuesday. Durst owns the 104-story building along with the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. The exact asking rent was not disclosed, but a source close to the deal told CO that the asking rent was in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. Durst was represented in-house by Eric Engelhardt, Karen Rose and Sayo Kamara, along with David Falk, Peter Shimkin, Hal Stein, Nathan Kropp and Paige Raisides from Newmark. Harry Singer from CBRE represented Mercor. The brokers did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “One World Trade Center was built for forward-looking, cutting-edge companies looking for a world-class home in New York City,” Jody Durst, president of Durst, said in a statement. “We look forward to welcoming Mercor to the building.” Mercor’s website says its headquarters is located in San Francisco, and does not list a New York City address. However, company CEO Brendan Foody previously posted on LinkedIn that the company would be expanding to New York City in January. It is unclear whether Foody was referencing the deal at One World Trade Center. “New York puts us close to some of the most important enterprises in the world, and to some of the highest caliber talent,” Koby Twist, special initiatives lead at Mercor, said in a statement. “One World Trade Center gives our team a home at the center of both.” One World Trade Center, the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, was built by the Port Authority and Durst in a public-private partnership. Other recent tenants include fashion and culture publication W Magazine, global financial services company Ameriprise Financial, and energy- and infrastruct
bullets: Mercor AI firm leases 25,550 SF at One World Trade Center, 77th floor; 5-year term
One WTC now ~100% leased; asking rents high $80s–$90s per SF
Manhattan office: tech/AI tenants still commanding premium space despite broader office headwinds
stories.summary: Mercor, an AI training firm, signed a five-year lease for 25,550 square feet on the 77th floor of One World Trade Center at asking rents in the high $80s to $90s per square foot. This deal pushes One WTC to approximately 100% occupancy. The Durst Organization owns the 104-story tower alongside the Port Authority.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, strong leasing velocity and rents at flagship Manhattan office towers (especially for AI/tech tenants) signal sustained talent migration and high-income employment in Lower Manhattan. This supports continued rental demand from office workers and executives in nearby residential markets, particularly luxury rentals in Battery Park, Tribeca, and the Financial District. Demonstrates pricing power remains for prime office space where tenants have high growth and capital.

stories.title: Inland to Develop Apartments near Mayo Clinic in Rochester
stories.excerpt: Inland National Development Company (INDC) plans to co-develop Citywalk Apartments, a 342-unit, market-rate multifamily community in downtown Rochester, Minnesota, directly across from Mayo Clinic’s downtown campus. “We are excited to bring Citywalk Apartments to the heart of downtown Rochester,” said Anthony Casaccio, president and CEO of INDC. “This project reflects our commitment to delivering high-quality housing in high-growth markets like Rochester, where demand continues to be driven by world-class healthcare and sustained economic expansion.” INDC and Reuter Walton Development are working together as co-developers of the project.The community will be strategically located at 201 6th Ave SW, directly across the street from Mayo Clinic’s new $5 billion expansion and within steps of Rochester’s pedestrian-friendly subway and skyway system. The project includes a mix of studio, one-, two-, and three-bedroom residences and 90 short-term, fully furnished rental units. Construction on Citywalk Apartments is expected to begin in 2026, with completion anticipated in 2028. Where the apartment industry connects. Meet the owners, investors, developers, lenders, brokers, and service providers driving today’s multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Build relationships, uncover opportunities, and stay ahead of what’s next. The post Inland to Develop Apartments near Mayo Clinic in Rochester appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Inland National Development + Reuter Walton plan 342-unit market-rate Citywalk Apartments; Rochester, MN
Located across from Mayo Clinic's $5B expansion; construction 2026–2028
Mix of studios, 1–3 BR plus 90 short-term furnished units; skyway-connected downtown site
stories.summary: Inland National Development Company and Reuter Walton Development are co-developing Citywalk Apartments, a 342-unit market-rate community in downtown Rochester, Minnesota, directly across from Mayo Clinic's new $5 billion campus expansion. The project will include studios through three-bedroom units plus 90 short-term furnished rentals, with construction starting in 2026 and delivery in 2028. The site sits within Rochester's pedestrian skyway system.
stories.impact: Rochester multifamily market is experiencing strong demand anchored by Mayo Clinic's $5B capital investment and ongoing medical employment growth. This signals sustained in-migration of healthcare workers, physicians, and allied professionals to the market. Developers should view Rochester as a high-confidence growth market for workforce housing; Mayo's expansion acts as a demand engine for mid-to-premium apartments. Additional development opportunities likely exist in adjacent submarkets and workforce/affordable tiers as the clinic campus grows.

stories.title: CBRE Arranges Sale of a 134K-SF Grocery-Anchored Retail Center in PA
stories.excerpt: CBRE has arranged the sale of Shops at Crossroads, a 133,717-square-foot grocery-anchored shopping center located at 3560 Route 611 in Bartonsville, Pennsylvania. Chris Munley, Colin Behr, Ryan Sciullo, and Casey Smith of CBRE’s National Retail Partners team represented the buyer and the seller in the transaction. Built in 2009, Shops at Crossroads is strategically located just off Interstate 80, along Route 611 in the Pocono region, with access to a commercial corridor that includes more than 4 million square feet of retail. The property is anchored by GIANT and features a mix of national and specialty tenants, including Visionworks, Red Lobster, Jersey Mike’s and CosmoProf. “Anchored by a dominant grocer with exceptional sales performance and contributing nearly half of the property’s NOI, the asset benefits from a highly stable income stream,” said Behr. “While Bartonsville is considered a tertiary market, the Route 611 corridor remains exceptionally dynamic, boasting approximately 99% retail occupancy and strong retailer demand.” The post CBRE Arranges Sale of a 134K-SF Grocery-Anchored Retail Center in PA appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Shops at Crossroads: 133,717 SF grocery-anchored retail, Bartonsville, PA; GIANT anchor, 99% occupancy
Route 611 corridor: 4M+ SF retail supply, I-80 access; tertiary market with strong retailer demand
Anchor GIANT generates ~50% of property NOI; stable income stream attracts institutional capital
stories.summary: CBRE arranged the sale of Shops at Crossroads, a 133,717-square-foot grocery-anchored retail center in Bartonsville, Pennsylvania, anchored by GIANT supermarket with tenants including Visionworks, Red Lobster, Jersey Mike's, and CosmoProf. Built in 2009, the property sits along Route 611 near I-80 in the Pocono region, where the retail corridor boasts ~4 million square feet of supply and 99% occupancy. The GIANT anchor drives nearly half the property's NOI.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance; retail-focused asset. However, the strong occupancy and institutional buyer interest in tertiary-market, grocery-anchored retail (especially with essential tenants like GIANT) reflects broader trend of investor capital flowing to non-discretionary-focused properties. This validates demand strength in secondary markets like the Poconos, which could signal nearby multifamily opportunities if population growth and labor market trends support it.

stories.title: Los Angeles’ Moribund Multifamily Market Shows Signs of Life
stories.excerpt: In a market where finding a development opportunity is akin to “finding a needle in a haystack,” and existing policy can “break the backs” of developers who want to build housing, it follows that a majority of the development community has “redlined” it to curtail future investment. It’s no surprise to hear that multifamily development in Los Angeles has become costlier, more tedious, and more rare. Developers face rising construction and labor costs, regulatory challenges and the city’s Measure ULA transfer tax, and persistent issues with financing. This year, only 8,500 units are expected to be added in the Los Angeles metro area, and rents will budge up just 1 percent, according to a recent Marcus & Millichap report — not exactly green flags for making big multifamily investments. But, despite the apparent doldrums hamstringing the housing sector, there have been signs of life and significant increases in certain types of multifamily asset sales. While quarterly multifamily sales by dollar amount hit a recent peak in mid-2025, per CBRE — and then fell by 50 percent in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the final quarter of 2025 — sales volume and the number of units sold has recently jumped in certain key categories. According to the Marcus & Millichap report, multifamily deal flow in the L.A. metro improved 25 percent in the year ending in March, with particular growth in Class C properties selling for $1 million to $5 million. Private investors are filling that gap, said John Chang, senior vice president and chief intelligence and analytics officer for Marcus & Millichap. Private investors closed 66 percent of the multifamily sales volume. 740 South Burnside Avenue, Los Angeles. PHOTO: Courtesy Marcus & Millichap “There’s always going to be a lot of demand to live here, and the economics of buying an existing property and trying to build somewhere are very, very different,” said Joshua Baum, founding principal of Hilgard Economics. “Inv
bullets: Los Angeles metro: only 8,500 units expected 2026; rents rising just 1% per Marcus & Millichap
Developer constraints: Measure ULA transfer tax, high construction/labor costs, financing headwinds, regulatory friction
Market showing early signs of life; select asset classes and deal types gaining traction despite doldrums
stories.summary: Los Angeles multifamily development remains highly constrained, with only 8,500 units projected to deliver in 2026 and rents rising just 1%, according to Marcus & Millichap. Developers cite rising construction and labor costs, Measure ULA transfer tax, regulatory challenges, and financing difficulties as key headwinds. However, recent data show early signs of recovery in select multifamily asset sales and deal types, suggesting some investor appetite is returning despite the difficult operating environment.
stories.impact: Los Angeles remains a cautionary tale for new development given restrictive policy, high costs, and weak absorption, but emerging signs of capital re-entry suggest selective opportunities may emerge in the next 12–18 months. Developers should monitor which asset types and geographies are seeing renewed institutional interest (likely affordable, adaptive reuse, or well-entitled shovel-ready projects). The 1% rent growth and low supply pipeline may eventually create pent-up demand, but near-term underwriting remains challenged by financing availability and policy risk.

stories.title: Centerforce Establishes Tacoma HQ, Buys Historic Bone Dry Shoe Building
stories.excerpt: Centerforce, a nonprofit organization that provides vocational training and employment services for adults with disabilities, has acquired the historic Bone Dry Shoe Building in downtown Tacoma for $2.5 million. The acquisition establishes a permanent headquarters for the growing nonprofit while preserving one of Tacoma’s most recognizable historic office buildings. Lee & Associates brokers John Bauder and Harrison Laird represented the seller in the transaction. Ryan Kershaw and Drew Frame of Kidder Mathews represented Centerforce in the acquisition. Originally constructed in 1917 for the Bone Dry Shoe Manufacturing Company, the building still showcases its original heavy timber framing, exposed concrete walls and hardwood floors. Centerforce plans to occupy a portion of the building as its corporate headquarters while continuing to lease the remaining office space. “Representing the owners in the sale of the Bone Dry Shoe Building has been especially meaningful because of the history behind both the property and the people who have called it home,” said Bauder. The post Centerforce Establishes Tacoma HQ, Buys Historic Bone Dry Shoe Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Centerforce nonprofit acquires historic Bone Dry Shoe Building, Tacoma, WA; $2.5M purchase price
1917-built office asset with heavy timber, concrete, hardwood; Centerforce HQ + lease remainder
Tacoma downtown office market: historic preservation, nonprofit/mission-driven capital, adaptive reuse trend
stories.summary: Centerforce, a nonprofit providing vocational training and employment services, acquired the historic Bone Dry Shoe Building in downtown Tacoma for $2.5 million. Originally constructed in 1917 for a shoe manufacturer, the building retains original timber framing, concrete, and hardwood. Centerforce will occupy a portion as corporate headquarters and lease out remaining office space.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, this signals Tacoma's downtown office market is attracting mission-driven and nonprofit capital, which can stabilize older buildings and neighborhoods. If additional nonprofits or social enterprises establish presence in downtown Tacoma alongside adaptive reuse conversions, it may drive neighborhood activation and eventual residential demand, particularly for workforce and supportive housing. Tacoma's relatively lower real estate costs compared to Seattle make it an emerging market for mixed-use and conversion plays.

stories.title: Lee & Associates Negotiates Purchase of 456K-SF Warehouse in Lansing
stories.excerpt: Lee & Associates of Illinois has negotiated the purchase of a 455,858-square-foot industrial building located at 16801 Exchange Avenue in Lansing, Illinois. Walter Murphy, Principal at Lee & Associates of Illinois, represented the buyer, RBT Industries (The Great Escape), a retailer of the leading home leisure products. RBT will occupy 313,982 square feet of the building, while the remainder of the building is leased to LKQ Corporation. The institutional seller, Plymouth Industrial REIT, Inc., was represented by Stephanie Park, Colin Green and Traci Payette of CBRE. The heavily powered building is close to I-80 and I-94. “The building’s location, potential future expansion, and the cooperation of Lansing in securing the Class 8 tax incentive made this acquisition their preferred choice,” said Murphy. Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Lee & Associates Negotiates Purchase of 456K-SF Warehouse in Lansing appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: RBT Industries (The Great Escape) purchases 455,858 SF warehouse, Lansing, IL; near I-80/I-94
RBT occupies 314K SF; LKQ Corporation leases remainder; Plymouth Industrial REIT seller
Buyer secured Class 8 tax incentive from Lansing; strong logistics corridor near Chicago
stories.summary: Lee & Associates negotiated the purchase of a 455,858-square-foot industrial building in Lansing, Illinois, for RBT Industries, a home leisure products retailer. RBT will occupy approximately 314,000 square feet, with LKQ Corporation leasing the remainder. The heavily powered facility sits near I-80 and I-94, and the buyer secured a Class 8 tax incentive from Lansing. Plymouth Industrial REIT was the institutional seller.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. However, the transaction confirms strong demand for last-mile/logistics facilities near Chicago's interstate corridors, suggesting continued population and job growth in the Chicagoland periphery. Tax incentives attracting occupier capital to Lansing may signal city investment in industrial/employment zones, which could indirectly support nearby multifamily if labor demand rises. Monitor broader Chicagoland industrial and logistics trends as a proxy for workforce in-migration.

stories.title: CRE Fundraising Reflects Distress Cycle That Never Arrived: Report
stories.excerpt: The long-awaited wave of distressed commercial real estate strategies has not materialized. That was the contrarian conclusion in a new Avison Young national capital markets brief released Tuesday showing that fundraising has not matched years of predictions for increased investments in distressed properties. The Avison Young research— shared exclusively with Commercial Observer — shows fundraising has shifted away from CRE strategies since 2022 from a prolonged elevated interest rate environment coupled with better-than-expected pricing levels for borrowers. While transaction volume is up year-over-year for distressed assets, capital is competing for a limited supply of opportunities due to less forced sales than previously envisioned, according to the report. ‘I think that the higher-for-longer sentiment is a part of it, but it’s also about a high level of competition that continues to push pricing and yield more to the borrower’s favor,” Marion Jones, executive managing director of U.S. Capital Markets at Avison Young, told Commercial Observer. Fundraising allocated for distressed or opportunistic strategies dipped 38 percent in 2023 to $51.9 billion from $83.5 billion the previous year, according to Avison Young data, as the Federal Reserve began to aggressively hike interest rates to combat inflation. After rising slightly to $59.7 billion in 2024, capital for distressed or opportunistic funds slid 65 percent to $20.9 billion in 2025. Distressed strategy fundraising has experienced a comeback so far in 2026 with $26 billion raised in the first half of the year, but the numbers are still way down from their 2022 peak. “It’s a great narrative on the buy side to say we’re participating in distressed opportunities in the marketplace but the actual pricing and conviction that investors have to have in order to consummate these transactions doesn’t necessarily mean that they’re buying them at really high cap rates or really low price per squar
bullets: Avison Young: CRE distress fundraising has not materialized despite years of predictions
Higher-for-longer rates + strong borrower pricing power reduced forced sales vs. 2022 expectations
Capital competing for limited distressed supply; transaction volume up but not forced liquidations
stories.summary: Avison Young research shows that anticipated waves of distressed CRE sales have not materialized. Despite elevated interest rates since 2022, borrower pricing power has remained stronger than predicted, resulting in fewer forced sales. While transaction volume is rising year-over-year for distressed assets, capital is competing for a limited supply of actual distressed opportunities, suggesting the distress cycle many predicted never fully arrived.
stories.impact: For multifamily developers, this means the anticipated wave of distressed acquisitions and off-market deals at deep discounts has not materialized. Cap rates and valuations remain elevated but stable, pricing out marginal underwriters. Developers should expect continued competitive bidding for quality assets and limited opportunistic purchasing windows. This reinforces the importance of strong balance sheets, patient capital, and long-term holds. It also suggests existing multifamily portfolios held better than feared, limiting the supply of discounted inventory.

stories.title: ACORE Capital Provides $140M to Refi Self-Storage Portfolio
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between the Ardent Companies and StepStone Real Estate has secured a $140 million loan to refinance a portfolio of eight, Class A self-storage properties in seven northeastern U.S. states, according to a release. Acore Capital provided the debt, structured as a floating-rate loan, while a JLL Capital Markets team of Brian Somoza, Steven Klein, John Bauman, Campbell Swango and Shishir Reddy arranged the transaction. JLL’s Klein noted in a statement that the institutional investment from ACORE Capital was attracted to the deal due to the supply-constraints of new self-storage across the northeastern U.S. and the experienced sponsorship in that asset class. “The combination of the Ardent Companies’ development expertise and these best-in-class facilities created a compelling value proposition that resonated strongly with lenders seeking quality exposure to the sector,” said Klein. Managed by third-party operator Extra Space Storage, the eight self-storage properties will feature 7,650 units and total 742,855 square feet of space. Each of the properties is climate-controlled and recently built, according to JLL. Thomas Olson, partner and head of self-storage strategy for the Ardent Companies, called the deal “an important milestone in the continued execution of our self-storage strategy” in a statement, and highlighted his firm’s commitment to the portfolio with StepStone Real Estate. “We remain focused on optimizing operations and creating long-term value through disciplined asset management and strategic growth,” he said. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: ACORE Capital provides $140M floating-rate refi for 8-property self-storage portfolio; northeastern US
Ardent Companies + StepStone JV; 7,650 units, 742,855 SF managed by Extra Space Storage
Lender cited supply constraints in northeast self-storage as key underwriting driver
stories.summary: A joint venture between Ardent Companies and StepStone Real Estate secured a $140 million floating-rate refinance loan from ACORE Capital for a portfolio of eight Class A self-storage properties across seven northeastern states. The properties will feature 7,650 units totaling 742,855 square feet and are managed by Extra Space Storage. JLL Capital Markets arranged the deal, noting lender appetite was driven by supply constraints in northeast self-storage.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact, though supply constraints in northeast self-storage (noted by lender) may indicate broader regional real estate tightness and strong competition for development capital. This reinforces that institutional lenders remain active for well-stabilized assets with experienced sponsors in constrained-supply submarkets. For multifamily, it signals northeast markets continue to attract capital; developers should monitor which subregions are seeing supply pressures across all property types, as these often precede multifamily demand acceleration.

stories.title: Integra Investments Buys West Palm Marina For $72M
stories.excerpt: The Huizenga family has sold a marina in West Palm Beach, Fla., to Integra Investments for $72.4 million, property records show. The asset, called Safe Harbor Rybovich, spans 15 acres at 4200 North Flagler Drive, between 38th and 44th streets, facing the Intracoastal Waterway. Last year, Huizenga Holdings filed plans to build a 2.6 million-square-foot, mixed-use development at the site, which officials approved. The proposal calls for the construction of four condo towers with 660 units in total as well as 20,691 square feet of office space, 37,445 square feet of retail, 14,376 square feet of restaurants, 12,750 square feet of crew amenities and 3,355 square feet of marine storage. Upland Debt Lender provided a $75 million floating-rate loan to multiple affiliates backed by different components of the future development, including a marina, retail space, condominiums and a parking garage. The project marks the first in West Palm Beach for Integra, a prominent Miami-based developer, and the latest for the city’s waterfront. Just north of the marina, Related Group completed the Icon Marina Village luxury rental complex in 2023. Other luxury condo projects in the pipeline include Related Ross’s Edgeworth, Shorecrest and South Flagler House, the last of which remains under construction, as well as Savanna’s Olara. Huizenga Holdings, led by H. Wayne Huizenga Jr., manages the Huizenga family’s private businesses and real estate. Late patriarch Wayne Huizenga founded AutoNation, among other companies, and had owned the Miami Dolphins and the Florida Panthers sports franchises. A representative for Huizenga Holdings could not be reached for comment, while a spokesperson for Integra has yet to provide comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Integra Investments purchases Safe Harbor Rybovich marina, West Palm Beach; $72.4M, 15 acres
Approved mixed-use: 660 condo units across 4 towers + 20K SF office, 37K SF retail, restaurants
Prior owner (Huizenga Holdings) obtained $75M floating-rate development loan for marina/condo/retail components
stories.summary: Integra Investments acquired Safe Harbor Rybovich marina in West Palm Beach for $72.4 million from the Huizenga family. The 15-acre waterfront site at 4200 North Flagler Drive faces the Intracoastal Waterway. Approved plans call for a 2.6 million-square-foot mixed-use development including four condo towers with 660 units, office, retail, restaurants, and marine amenities. Upland Debt provided a $75 million floating-rate construction loan.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach waterfront remains a high-demand development corridor with strong institutional capital and financing availability for large mixed-use projects. The 660-unit condo tower component signals robust demand for owner-occupied waterfront housing, likely pulling in high-income buyers and reducing rental demand in that specific segment. For multifamily developers, this confirms West Palm's continued desirability but suggests competitive pressure in the luxury/high-end segment. Monitor whether Integra's development drives further waterfront appreciation and job growth that could support rental demand in adjacent, more affordable submarkets.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Ponce-Davis: $18.9M mansion sale; telecom exec buyer, 10K SF on 1 acre, completed 2026
Wynwood commercial: $24.5M sale of 13K SF mixed-use property; Pastis/Apparatus tenants
Jupiter waterfront: $10.5M home sale; $1,500/SF; cell tower executive buyer; traded at $8M in 2022
stories.summary: South Florida recorded high-value transactions in July 2026: a $18.9 million newly built mansion in Ponce-Davis sold to a telecom executive; a $24.5 million Wynwood property (13K SF mixed-use, home to Pastis and Apparatus) sold to investor Moishe Mana; and a $10.5 million waterfront home in Jupiter sold at ~$1,500/SF, up from $8M in 2022.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. However, strong luxury residential and mixed-use pricing in Miami/Palm Beach markets, coupled with robust commercial demand in Wynwood, signal sustained high-income population in-migration and investment appetite in South Florida. Workforce housing and middle-market multifamily in secondary submarkets (further inland, lower-cost cities) may benefit from wealth effect and tax migration. Developers should evaluate value-add and workforce housing opportunities in growing south-of-the-border submarkets less impacted by luxury price escalation.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing + Capstone Equities: 316 affordable units, Warner Center, Woodland Hills, LA; 7 stories
Demolish 35K SF commercial building; Executive Directive 1 expedited approval pathway
Part of broader Warner Center redevelopment anchored by LA Rams HQ (Stan Kroenke 52-acre mixed-use)
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street in Woodland Hills to develop 316 affordable units (The Green at Warner Center) on a 2.7-acre site. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building will be demolished, and the seven-story affordable project will use LA's Executive Directive 1 for expedited approvals. The development is part of the broader Warner Center revitalization anchored by Stan Kroenke's Rams headquarters and 52-acre mixed-use master plan.
stories.impact: Warner Center is rapidly becoming an affordable housing and mixed-use hotspot within LA, driven by Kroenke's $5B+ investment in the Rams campus and adjacent mixed-use. The convergence of institutional capital (Capstone, Elysian) targeting affordable development, streamlined permitting (Directive 1), and major anchor tenant investment suggests this San Fernando Valley submarket will see sustained multifamily absorption and job growth. Developers should view Warner Center as a high-conviction target for workforce and affordable housing given entitlement ease, public investment, and private capital momentum. Conversions and land acquisition opportunities may exist nearby before land costs fully reflect future rents.

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill, TX (Austin suburb) selects Griffin Swinerton for downtown revamp; 2028 launch target
City tripled in size since 2020; fast-growing suburb experiencing massive population boom
Joint venture will manage community engagement, infrastructure planning, financing evaluation, walkability focus
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, one of the nation's fastest-growing Austin-area suburbs, hired Griffin Swinerton (a joint venture of Griffin Structures and Swinerton) to lead a downtown revitalization project. The developer will serve as city staff, handling community engagement, infrastructure evaluation, and financing for the master plan. A 2028 start date is targeted. Liberty Hill has tripled in size since the 2020 census and continues rapid population growth.
stories.impact: Liberty Hill represents a high-growth suburban corridor with pent-up demand for downtown mixed-use and residential development. The appointment of Griffin Swinerton signals city intent to accelerate entitlements and infrastructure investment, reducing development friction. Developers should evaluate land acquisition opportunities in Liberty Hill's downtown and adjacent corridors before Griffin Swinerton's planning efforts drive land value appreciation. The tripling of population since 2020 and ongoing migration to the Austin metro suggest strong multifamily and mixed-use fundamentals; early-mover developers can capture upside as downtown becomes focal point of community growth.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK Companies: 910 South Wells, 28-story South Loop high-rise; 350 units + 3,300 SF retail; piles permit secured
Part of Riverline master plan (8 acres, $2B vision, halved after Lendlease split 2018)
CMK secured $83M construction loan from Wintrust Bank for first phase (1010 South Wells, 388 units, under construction)
stories.summary: The CMK Companies secured a piles permit for 910 South Wells in Chicago's South Loop, a 28-story mixed-use tower designed by Gensler with 350 residential units and 3,300 square feet of retail. The building is part of CMK's eight-acre Riverline master plan (originally a $2 billion vision reduced after the company's 2018 partnership split with Lendlease). CMK recently secured an $83 million construction loan from Wintrust Bank to fund the first phase, which includes a 388-unit apartment building at 1010 South Wells Street, already under construction.
stories.impact: Chicago South Loop remains a high-velocity multifamily development corridor with strong institutional financing support and continued absorption. CMK's multi-phase, multi-building strategy (1010 and 910 South Wells) signals confidence in sustained demand; the Wintrust construction financing validates lender appetite for well-capitalized, experienced developers in prime urban markets. For competing developers, this highlights that entitlement and financing windows remain open in South Loop, but land cost and permitting pace are accelerating. Developers should prioritize shovel-ready sites and consider joint ventures or capital partnerships to compete with CMK's size and track record.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas: $800M wellness resort, 500K SF, 24 acres; skybridge addition, 2027 groundbreaking target
Austrian company faced controversy: misrepresented development experience to Ontario; only one completed spa (Bucharest)
Trinity River waterfront site; retractable glass roof, thermal baths, swim-up bars; adjacent Water Commons wetlands
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an Austrian wellness company, is pursuing city approval for design modifications to its $800 million, 500,000-square-foot resort on 24 acres along Trinity River. The additions include a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones. A 2027 groundbreaking is targeted. However, Therme faced controversy when a New York Times investigation revealed the company misrepresented its development experience to Ontario authorities; it has only completed one spa (Bucharest), not the six globally-placed facilities claimed.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact, though the Therme project's location on the Trinity River reflects broader Dallas waterfront redevelopment momentum (Harold Simmons Park, Water Commons). The developer's credibility questions and history of misrepresentation introduce execution and financing risk; institutional capital and lenders may require enhanced due diligence. For multifamily developers monitoring Dallas growth, the Therme project's slow approval pace and design pivots suggest permitting friction remains, even for marquee anchor tenants. Focus on proven developers with track records in the Dallas market.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development: $28M construction financing for The Albritton mixed-use, Friendswood, TX; S3 Capital lender
111 multifamily units + 11K SF retail + 9K SF office; 70% LTV; completion 2028
Friendswood: 25 miles SE Houston; Tannos also building Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106 acres) + Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres)
stories.summary: Tannos Development Group secured $28 million in mid-construction financing from S3 Capital (arranged by Concord Summit Capital) for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas. The 70% LTV loan carries financing through project completion in 2028. The Albritton includes 111 multifamily units, 11,000 square feet of retail, and 9,000 square feet of office. Friendswood is located 25 miles southeast of Downtown Houston and sits between Houston and Galveston.
stories.impact: Friendswood and adjacent Houston suburbs are experiencing significant multifamily and mixed-use development activity, signaling sustained metro population in-migration and job growth. Tannos' simultaneous pipeline of three major projects (The Albritton, Friendswood City Center $750M, Magnolia Town Center $1B) demonstrates developer confidence and institutional lender appetite in the Greater Houston market. Financing availability and 70% LTV on mid-stage construction loans validates market strength. Developers should monitor Friendswood and Magnolia corridors for emerging workforce housing opportunities as these large master-planned communities mature; land costs and entitlements are more favorable than core Houston submarkets.
